card.intuition: When the optimal split point opt[i] is monotone (opt[i] ≤ opt[i+1]), the O(n²) DP inner loop can be replaced with divide-and-conquer: solve the midpoint, then recurse on each half knowing the opt range is bounded — reducing to O(n log n).
card.analogy: Narrowing a search for the cheapest bus route: instead of checking all possible transfer cities for every destination, you discover that the best transfer city only ever moves right as the destination moves right — halving the search space each time.
card.time_complexity: O(n log n) when opt monotonicity holds; vs O(n²) naïve
card.space_complexity: O(n) for one DP row + O(log n) recursion stack
when_to_use: 1D DP recurrence dp[i] = min over j < i of (dp[j] + cost(j, i)) when cost satisfies concave SMAWK / divide-and-conquer opt monotonicity
Optimal k-partition problems (k-means on sorted data, binomial DP)
Knuth's O(n²) interval DP optimisation (special case)
gotchas: Only valid when the optimal split point is monotone — verify this before applying
The cost function must satisfy the quadrangle inequality or similar condition for opt monotonicity
Divide-and-conquer optimisation is different from divide-and-conquer DP — the former is an O(n log n) optimisation of a 1D DP

exercise.prompt: Implement the divide-and-conquer DP optimisation for a 1D DP with monotone opt.
exercise.starter_code:
def dc_dp(n, cost):
    """
    Computes dp[i] = min over j in [0, i) of (dp[j] + cost(j, i))
    using divide-and-conquer on the assumption that opt is monotone.
    cost: callable cost(j, i)
    """
    INF = float('inf')
    dp  = [INF] * (n + 1)
    dp[0] = 0

    def solve(lo, hi, opt_lo, opt_hi):
        if lo > hi:
            return
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        best_cost = INF
        best_opt  = opt_lo
        for j in range(opt_lo, min(opt_hi, mid - 1) + 1):
            c = dp[j] + cost(j, mid)
            if c < best_cost:
                best_cost = c
                best_opt  = j
        dp[mid] = best_cost
        solve(lo,      mid - 1, opt_lo,   best_opt)  # left half: opt in [opt_lo, best_opt]
        solve(mid + 1, hi,      ___________,  opt_hi)  # right half: opt in [best_opt, opt_hi]

    solve(1, n, 0, n - 1)
    return dp[n]
hints: For the right half (indices > mid), the optimal split point is at least best_opt — pass best_opt as the lower bound of the opt range
exercise.test_code:
# dp[i] = min over j<i of (dp[j] + (i-j)^2)  (convex cost => monotone opt)
def cost(j, i): return (i - j) ** 2
result = dc_dp(5, cost)
# Brute force for verification
INF = float('inf')
dp_bf = [INF] * 6; dp_bf[0] = 0
for i in range(1, 6):
    for j in range(i):
        dp_bf[i] = min(dp_bf[i], dp_bf[j] + cost(j, i))
assert result == dp_bf[5], f'Got {result}, expected {dp_bf[5]}'
print('PASS')
